meta.title: A Journey Through Infertility
meta.description: An interactive journey about IVF, told from two perspectives.
meta.keywords: ivf, fertility, pregnancy, baby, mom, mother, in vitro fertilization, assisted reproductive technology, hormones, birth, reproduction, women, woman, personal, data, essay

byline2: With Jan Diehm and Michelle Pera-McGhee
byline3: Illustrations by Rose Wong

landing.text: This is an interactive story about In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) told from two perspectives:

text.value: I never thought becoming a mom was going to be an issue for me. I came from a fertile group of women: my paternal grandmother had 13 children, and my mother’s mom had 6.
But alas, I was not able to become pregnant after months of trying, and my doctor sent me on the route of IVF.

mom.description: An illustrated person walks onscreen, onto a winding path.

text.value: IVF is an obstacle course of logistics that you have to overcome while living your day-to-day life.
For the past two years, IVF has tested my physical and emotional stamina. It has eaten into my savings. And I’ve been heartbroken again and again.

mom.description: She walks through a heart, which breaks in two as she passes.

text.value: Many women who’ve done IVF call themselves warriors.
And I can see why: This whole journey has felt like a quest where I’ve had to put on a brave face and don armor as I took on one challenge after another.

mom.description: She dons armor, like a warrior.

text.value: First, the five egg retrievals.

To try and produce as many eggs as my body could muster, I became a science experiment: I injected myself with a cocktail of hormones meant to mirror the body’s natural reproductive signals, gave my doctor a blood sample every other day, and had an ultrasound technician monitor my ovaries.

mom.definition-retrievals: An egg retrieval is a procedure that takes between 15 and 30 minutes during which a doctor removes mature eggs from a person’s ovaries.
mom.definition-cocktail: The synthetic hormones are meant to encourage the production of multiple eggs, prevent the premature release of eggs or ovulation and optimize the timing of the egg retrieval.
mom.description: She covers her head with her hands as needles descend on her.

text.value: Over the course of five retrievals, I was stabbed with 932 needles.

deep-needles.value: My veins were poked 79 times for blood samples.

value.alt: a grid of 79 illustrated yellow needles

deep-needles.value: I stabbed myself with 166 syringes to self-inject hormones.

value.alt: a grid of 166 illustrated coral needles

deep-needles.value: And because I wanted to improve my odds, I went to 31 acupuncture appointments, where 687 needles pierced my underbelly, legs and head. My doctors had varying opinions on how much acupuncture is helpful, but my acupuncturists told me it helped with hormone regulation and blood flow.

value.alt: a grid of 687 illustrated purple needles

mom.description: She covers her head with her hands as more needles descend on her.

mom.text: Each cycle was also a test of my ability to handle logistics. There were the 7 a.m. doctor’s appointments before work; the dozens of days working from home in order to take all my medications; and the many times I reshuffled my travel plans. I’ve become a master of schedule Tetris.

deep-logistics.value: Over 20 months, I navigated 100+ appointments, averaging one per week. During my six busiest months (including late 2024 and much of 2025), I averaged 10+ visits a month—meaning I spent one out of every three days in a doctor’s office.

value.title: My appointments calendar
value.alt: a calendar heatmap of doctor’s appointments

mom.description: She walks past a big calendar showing a chaotic mess of appointments.

text.value: There are adversaries, too: the insurance agent who wrongfully denied my claim; the person from the finance department who overcharged me multiple times; and the administrator who wanted $7 to release my digital health records.

deep-claim.value: According to the Kaiser Family Foundation, 23 states (including DC) require private insurance companies to cover fertility-related treatment, though the details may vary.

value.title: Private insurance fertility coverage
value.alt: a U.S. map where states are colored by if private insurance covers (yellow, 23 states) or does not cover (purple, 28 states) fertility-related treatment

deep-claim.value: And 18 states (including DC) require Medicaid to cover fertility-related treatment, though the specific services and rules can differ depending on which private insurance company manages your Medicaid plan.

value.title: Medicaid fertility coverage
value.alt: a U.S. map where states are colored by if medicaid covers (yellow, 18 states) or does not cover (purple, 33 states) fertility-related treatment

mom.description: She is surrounded by icons representing these adversaries.

text.value: There’s also so much that can go wrong.
One time, my fertility clinic did not send in my prescription on time. I had to call the pharmacy 32 times to get my medication approved.

mom.text: Another time, I was sent to the wrong clinic for an appointment.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and when she reaches the edge of the path, the floor ahead falls out from underneath her.

text.value: And yet another time, I received a text message that falsely claimed I had $10,000 in medical debt. All this, while I was still sobbing over a failed IVF cycle.

deep-debt.value: IVF costs can be inconsistent and unpredictable. During my own journey, I saw prices that ranged from $5,000 to $26,000 for a single cycle. There are so many variables that impact the price point: if you have insurance (and if it covers fertility treatments and what your deductible is); what kinds of medicine and test you need; if you chose to do ICSI (Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection) or PGT-A (Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Aneuploidy); how many cycles you have; even where you live.

The costs can be bankrupting. The Kaiser Family Foundation reports that among women age 18–49 who needed fertility services 12% of them did not receive them because of cost. That number doubles to 24%, or one in four, for low-income women.

value.title: Use of fertility services by women who need them
value.alt: a dot plot showing the difference between lower income and high income use of fertility services by women who needed them: fertility advice (lower: 33%, higher: 59%); fertility testing (lower: 25%, higher: 56%); ovulation drugs (lower: 28%, higher:  44%); IVF (lower: 8%, higher: 17%); IUI (lower 4%, higher: 18%); surgery/drugs for other conditions (lower: 9%, higher: 14%); egg freezing (lower: 5%, higher: 9%)
value.credit: SOURCE: Kaiser Family Foundation, lower income defined as women with incomes at or below 200% of the federal poverty level, higher income defined as women with incomes at or above 200% of the federal poverty level

mom.description: She continues to walk, and bills pile up alongside the path.

mom.text: I’ve persevered for so long, spent so much money, and arrived at every appointment on time.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and buries her face in her hands, crying.

text.value: And even after I did everything right, I was still foiled by bad luck, a dark cloud.

The majority of my embryos were abnormal, leaving me with just 4 that were viable. I’ve done 3 transfers so far, and while each failed to implant, I still have one remaining. Maybe it will be the one that results in a pregnancy.

mom.description: She cries and walks, and a cloud appears over her head.

text.value: I have had small wins that kept me going, and I’ve lost big when I felt I was close to the goal.
Sometimes it feels like I’m stuck, while the world keeps moving on. Six friends have gotten pregnant since I started this journey — two of them through IVF.

mom.text: But I’ve also met so many people on my quest who’ve pushed ahead for me when I couldn’t muster the strength.
mom.description: She continues to walk, looking hopeful.

mom.text: There is Simone, a friend and fellow IVF warrior, who shared so many little crucial tips and helped me keep going.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and a friendly figure appears hovering above her.

mom.text: There are my other fellow warriors: a friend and her wife who conceived their daughter through IVF; a friend’s wife who sent me a gift during a tender time; a friend who’s freezing her eggs to preserve her fertility. We walked alongside one another.
There are the many women on Reddit, on Facebook and in Google Groups, who encourage each other, share knowledge, and console one another.

deep-encourage.value: IVF-related reddit groups Facebook groups abound online. They can be as specific to your condition, like “IVF SUPPORT own eggs 40+” (for women who are 40 and older and want to use their own eggs) or “IVF with Endometriosis,” or they can be location-specific. While it can be a lot to read about and hold the collective anxieties of dozens of strangers, I found that the posts asking for stories of successful pregnancies to be the sweetest.

“Just need some encouragement here and was wondering if anyone here has any success stories? Thank you Ladies ❤️” one user asked after sharing her story. The responses are full of specifics of each person’s journey but also full of expressions of care.

value.value: Sorry to hear you’re in pain.
I can understand deeply the longing to find success stories of women in your shoes and how hard it is to hold onto hope. Hang in there!
Everyone’s story is going to be different but there is hope for everyone who has a uterus and ovaries.
fertility treatment is hard. Take care ♥️

text.value: I’ve built my own little band of warriors. We cheer each other on. It’s a shitty club none of us want to be part of, but one that is full of the best people.

mom.text: This feels like a lonely journey. But in the end, there’s a whole village cheering us on and ready to care for us when the time comes.

beats.description: She walks through a door, comes out the other side, and meets the child, who has journeyed from the other side, in the middle.

text.value: My story begins as a cell.
With a lot of help and a little luck, I’ll develop into an egg, then an embryo, and eventually a child.
My mom struggles with infertility, so she started in vitro fertilization or IVF to give me a boost.

baby.description: An illustrated follicle rolls onscreen, onto a winding path.

baby.text: Right now, I’m tucked inside one of her follicles. It’s a busy neighborhood here in the ovaries, and dozens of us are waiting for our chance. A follicle’s job is to grow and nourish an egg until it is ready to be released. Typically, only one dominant follicle releases an egg every month. Every time a neighbor leaves, I wave goodbye, wish them luck, and then watch as a new follicle settles in.
baby.definition-follicles: Ovarian follicles are sacs filled with fluid that have the potential to release an egg. Follicles are often used to assess fertility and fertility treatment.
baby.description: Several more follicles appear surrounding it.

baby.text: The odds of one lonely egg becoming a healthy child are slim, especially since my mom was in her late 30s when her journey began. As hard as she and my dad tried, the eggs she released each month did not end up in a pregnancy.
baby.definition-egg: The human egg, or ovum, together with sperm, is vital to human reproduction. Women are born with about 1-2 million eggs which they release over their lifetime. Each month, women of reproductive age usually release one egg.
baby.description: The follicles travel along the path.

deep-births.value: If you’re like my mom, you’re constantly bombarded with questions about your “biological clock.” A 2017 study used the fecundability ratio—the monthly likelihood of conception—to measure how much faster it ticks for women. While a man's odds of conceiving remain relatively stable from age 20 to 45, women face a much steeper curve: a 45-year-old woman has less than half the monthly chance of conceiving compared to a 21-year-old.

value.title: Monthly likelihood of conception
value.alt: a line chart showing the monthly likelihood of conception, or fecundability, by age and gender. as men age, they have roughly the same odds of conception as they did when they were 21, while women’s chances drop off as they age — a 45-year-old woman has less than half the chance of conception compared to a 21-year-old woman.
value.credit: SOURCE: Age and fecundability in a North American preconception cohort study, 2017

deep-births.value: Despite these physiological hurdles, birth rates for women ages 35–39 have risen over the last decade, while rates for those in their teens and early twenties have fallen.

value.title: Births per 1,000 women by age
value.alt: a line chart showing births per 1,000 women by age between 2010–2022. women ages 35–39 have seen an increase in births, while women 15–19 and 20–24 have seen a decline.

deep-births.value: Some of this increase can be attributed to Assistive Reproductive Technology (ART), which includes treatments like IVF. Between 2013 and 2022, the number of ART cycles performed in the U.S. more than doubled. Today, the average age of a woman utilizing these technologies is 36.3 years.

value.title: Assistive Reproductive Cycles (ART)
value.alt: a line chart showing the rise in art cycles from 2013–2022. in 2013, there were about 190,000 cycles. by 2022, that number had more than doubled to roughly 435,000 cycles.

deep-births.value: But age creates challenges for IVF as well. While IVF success rates using donor eggs remain steady at roughly 40% for women from age 30 through 45, the success rate for those using their own eggs drops significantly as they age, plummeting to just 10% by age 45.

value.title: Embryo transfers that result in live-births by age
value.alt: a line chart showing the percentage of embryo transfers that result in a live birth. ivf success rates using donor eggs remain steady at roughly 40% for women from age 30 through 45, but the success rate for those using their own eggs drops significantly as they age, plummeting to just 10% by age 45

baby.text: That’s where IVF can help. Instead of following the “one at a time” rule, a whole group of us were able to mature at once. The more eggs, the better the odds, and the more goodbye waves I had to give.

deep-ivf.value: An IVF cycle follows these steps:

value.title: Ovarian stimulation
value.text: To start the IVF process, women are instructed to inject hormones every day into their lower abdomen to encourage the growth of as many follicles as possible to produce as many eggs as possible.
value.alt: an icon of a needle piercing skin

value.title: Egg retrieval
value.text: Once the eggs are mature (roughly 2 weeks after your period started), they are retrieved by a doctor.
value.alt: an icon eggs inside a test jar

value.title: Egg fertilization
value.text: An embryologist fertilizes each egg, and then observes them for 5-6 days. Not all eggs fertilize to become embryos or make it to the 5th day, but the ones that are of higher quality are then generally frozen.
value.alt: an icon eggs inside a test jar

value.title: Embryo transfer
value.text: One of the embryos is then transferred back into the uterus of the willing parent (this also involves lots of hormone shots and pills).
value.alt: an icon of an embryo implanted in the uterus

value.title: 2-week wait
value.text: You then have to wait 2 weeks (14 days) to take a pregnancy test and see if the embryo successfully implanted. This wait can be grueling, especially after you’ve come so far.
value.alt: an icon of a calendar page with the number 14 on it

baby.description: The follicles travel along the path.

baby.text: During her first round of IVF, my mom started with 8 follicles. After 14 days of hormonal injections, 7 produced eggs.
All 7 of those eggs were mature enough to be retrieved by her doctor.
And, 6 of those eggs were successfully fertilized by an embryologist. For 5 days, they were a small, hopeful group.
baby.description: 6 of the eggs are successfully fertilized.

baby.text: An embryologist watched the embryos grow into a more mature stage called a blastocyst, but only 3 of them made it and were graded.
baby.definition-blastocyst: A blastocyst is a cluster of cells that forms after an egg is fertilized. It’s the early stage of an embryo.

deep-grading.value: Embryos are graded using 3 criteria and given 3-digit alpha-numeric codes.

value.value: Blastocyst expansion (first number): graded from 1 to 6, with 6 being the most expanded from the embryo
Inner cell mass (middle letter): graded from A to C, with A being the highest quality with the most tightly packed cells
Trophectoderm (last letter): graded from A to C, with A being the highest quality with many cells forming a cohesive layer

value.title: Day 5 embryo grading
value.alt: a heatmap showing day 5 embryo grading. excellent embryos are concentrated in the mid left with classifications like 3AA, 3AB, 4AA, 4AB, 5AA, 5AB

deep-grading.value: Embryos with higher grades have a stronger chance of implantation, while those with lower grades are more fragile.

value.title: IVF transfer outcome by grade
value.alt: 2 bars charts showing the pregnancy rates by embryo grade (excellent: 65%, good: 59%, average: 50%, poor: 33%) and live birth rates by embryo grade (excellent: 50%, good: 50%, average: 42%, poor: 25%)

baby.description: The eggs turn into blastocysts, and only 3 make it.

text.value: Then we face another crucial hurdle: genetic testing. Since my mom is older than 35, doctors recommend these tests.
After an agonizingly long 21-day wait, we learned that all 3 of my mom’s embryos were abnormal and would likely lead to miscarriages if implanted.

baby.definition-genetictest: PGTA (Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Aneuploidy) testing checks an embryo for abnormalities in the number of chromosomes. Health professionals and hopeful parents have varying opinions about the efficacy and ethics of this testing.
baby.description: The 3 blastocysts are no longer viable, and turn grey.

text.value: To me, IVF seems like a cruel lottery system drawn out over weeks.
Each cycle takes about 40 days from start to finish. If it doesn’t result in an implantable embryo, you start over. Back to a follicle.

baby.description: Back to one follicle, which continues down the path.

text.value: My mom braved this process 5 times, from follicle, to egg, to fertilized egg, to embryo.

deep-times.value: In total, my mom produced 57 eggs. Of the resulting embryos, 21 developed into blastocysts, and 4 were confirmed to have the correct number of chromosomes and were suitable to transfer as euploids. But, heartbreakingly, none of them implanted.

value.title: Cycle outcomes
value.alt: a funnel visual for each of the 5 IVF cycles. cycle 1: 7 eggs, 3 blastocytes, 0 euploids; cycle 2: 9 eggs, 4 blastocytes, 0 euploids; cycle 3: 14 eggs, 7 blastocytes, 2 euploids; cycle 4: 18 eggs, 4 blastocytes, 2 euploids; cycle 5: 9 eggs, 3 blastocytes, 0 euploids

text.value: When it was finally time to transfer an embryo, my mom underwent a battery of tests to make sure the new neighborhood in her uterus was ready for them.

Everything looked clear, but the first embryo didn’t implant. Neither did the next. That’s when my mom learned about her endometriosis—a condition that made her uterus a lot less hospitable than we’d hoped. The next “move-in day” transfer was delayed.

baby.definition-endometriosis: Endometriosis is a condition that causes tissue similar to the uterine-lining to grow in other parts of the body, leading to inflammation and scar tissue. It can cause pain, heavy periods, and is a common cause of infertility.

text.value: It’s hurdles like these — the abnormal embryos, the failed transfers, the silent losses — that make this journey feel like an uphill climb for people like my mom. She’s doing everything she can to help one little egg make it to birth.
Thankfully, she’s not alone.
Yes, there are the doctors, nurses, sonographers and other clinical staffers who are helping as much as they can. But there are others, too.

baby.description: One embryo continues down the path.

baby.text: There was the nutritionist who coached my mom on what to eat to help make a healthy embryo.
baby.description: The embryo continues on, and a friendly figure appears above it.

baby.text: There was the fertility doula—part strategist, part shield. She helped my mom interpret each test result and helped her find a better clinic. She even went to battle with the finance departments alongside my mom, saving thousands of dollars.
There were the acupuncturists who channeled a lot of medicinal wisdom from East Asia and adjusted their treatment to each phase of my mom’s journey.
And there was the microbiologist who wrote a whole book in which she laid out what supplements and foods may be helpful.

Even with the help of all these people, along with specialized treatments for her endometriosis and one more transfer, she’s still fighting to get pregnant. I hope to be the one who will finally help my mom conceive.
baby.description: The embryo continues along with the 4 figures hovering above them.

baby.text: This feels like a lonely journey. But in the end, there’s a whole village cheering us on and ready to care for us when the time comes.
baby.description: The child walks through a door, comes out the other side, and meets the mom, who has journeyed from the other side, in the middle.

methods.value: Thank you to my village. Thank you to my husband and family who’ve held me during each failed cycle.

Thank you to the nurses, clinical writers, receptionists and the doctors at Generation Next Fertility, the clinic I found after some terrible experiences elsewhere. I didn’t catch the names of every nurse or staffer there, but would like to give a shoutout to Abigail, Kendall, Yamika, Abigail, Jennifer, Sophia, Alex, Elizabeth and Joana. Thank you, Dr. Janelle Luk, for listening to what I want and for taking on my tricky case, thank you to my OBGYN Dr. Lindsay Lauren Goodman, for letting me play Bad Bunny in the lead up to my endometriosis surgery to feel less scared, and thank you to Ms. Lee, my acupuncturist who’s been so gentle with me throughout.

Thanks to Laura Kradas, my fertility advocate and rock, and to all my friends, Aggi, Giulia, Simone, Atlas, MR, Jon, Tra, Hanna, Rachael, Taylor, Jessie, Sienna, Naina, Naina’s sister Rohini, Naina’s mom Nitu, Julia, Nabiha, Sabrina, Teon, Mazin, Carla, Zahra, and so many more, who through their own journeys, some of them as queer parents, helped me learn how to reframe parenthood and know that love is love, who gave me advice, who sent me care packages, who whisked me away on adventures to distract me, and who firmly believe in my future as a mother, either through birth or through other means.

The idea for parallel paths and the illustration style developed independently, but the team did take inspiration for the isometric world and user experience mechanics from the game Monument Valley once made aware. If you enjoyed this format, give Monument Valley a play through the Apple or Android app stores.
And, if you enjoyed this story please consider giving author Lam Thuy Vo a tip! 100% of your donation goes directly to them.

bio.value: Lam Thuy Vo is a data journalism professor at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism and an investigative reporter at Documented. When they aren’t crunching data, they spend their time making bespoke ceramics for their friends or teaching others how to play with clay on the wheel. You can follow them on their socials here and check out their website here.
